event: Saint Paul and the Shipwreck on Malta
symbols: bonfire on shore — warmth and survival after the shipwreck, hospitality of the Maltese
snake near the fire — the viper that bit Paul but caused no harm, proving divine protection
shipwreck debris at left — the destruction of the vessel but preservation of all souls
Paul's raised arms — prayerful thanksgiving and apostolic authority even in crisis
stormy sky — the divine providence that works through catastrophe
description: A dramatic scene on a stormy shore. A group of survivors from a shipwreck gathers around a bonfire on a beach. At center, a tall figure in white raises his arms in prayer or thanksgiving. Other figures in colorful classical dress attend to the injured — a woman holds a child at the left, others gesture in distress. A broken ship is visible in the turbulent sea at the far left. A snake appears near the fire at the lower right. Trees bend in the wind above. The sky is dark and threatening with a flash of light breaking through.
theological_description: Paul's shipwreck on Malta (Acts 27-28) is one of the most vivid narratives in the New Testament, demonstrating that God's purposes cannot be thwarted by storm, sea, or serpent. During the perilous voyage to Rome, Paul received a divine assurance that all 276 souls aboard would be saved (Acts 27:22-24). When they reached Malta, a viper bit Paul's hand as he gathered wood for a fire — but he shook it off unharmed, astonishing the islanders who expected him to swell up and die. The Maltese concluded he must be a god; Paul used the opportunity to heal the sick and preach the Gospel. The entire episode illustrates Romans 8:28: 'All things work together for good for those who love God.' What appeared to be catastrophe became the occasion for the Gospel's advance — a pattern repeated throughout the history of the Church.

scripture_refs.reason: Paul's divine assurance that all aboard the ship would survive the storm

They will pick up serpents and not be harmed — fulfilled in Paul's experience on Malta
